text.content: MINI PRD: P128 — Aged Stainless Steel C-Values
Status: Plan
Batch: 13 | Priority: P3 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What This Article Does Now
This is a NEW article.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Guide pharmaceutical engineers through modeling pressure drop in aging stainless steel piping where surface roughness increases over decades of service, affecting system performance and potentially invalidating original design calculations. Must open with the validation challenge (proving existing systems still meet specs), explain why Hazen-Williams C-values degrade for stainless, then show how FluidFlow models aged pipe performance using degraded roughness or C-values.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the validation problem — pharmaceutical facilities with 15-30 year old stainless steel piping must prove systems still deliver specified flows and pressures, but surface roughness increases with age, particularly in high-purity water systems.
**Pain point focus**: Revalidation requirements for aging facilities, pressure loss increases over time, FDA concerns about system suitability, capital cost of repipe vs operational margins.

**FluidFlow capability map**: Liquid Module with Hazen-Williams correlation (C-value adjustment) or Darcy-Weisbach with pipe scaling factor, pressure drop comparison between new and aged conditions, system curve analysis for pump performance verification.

**Practical scenario**: 20-year-old 316L PW loop, compare new pipe (C=140-150) vs aged pipe (C=110-120), calculate pressure drop increase, verify pump still operates on curve, determine if repiping is justified.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting aged pipe modeling to revalidation success and capital planning.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Hazen-Williams](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#hazen-williams) — in C-value discussion
[Roughness](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#roughness) — in aging pipe section
[System Curve](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#system-curve) — in pump performance verification
[C-Factor](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#c-factor) — in Hazen-Williams correlation section
[Pressure Gradient](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#pressure-gradient) — in aged pipe analysis
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview
SIDEWAYS: P121 PW Loop Velocity, P126 Duty/Standby Pump Range, P122 WFI Loop NPSH, P131 Potable Water Pumps
DOWN (glossary): Hazen-Williams, Roughness, System Curve, C-Factor, Pressure Gradient
CTA: Contact Us — https://fluidflowinfo.com/contact
Selling Message
4 — "Validated by 40+ years" — FluidFlow's decades of pharmaceutical project experience inform realistic aging factors. Weave naturally into revalidation discussion.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Liquid Module: Hazen-Williams correlation (C-value adjustment) or Darcy-Weisbach with scaling
Pipe scaling: Applied to account for pipe degradation/current status
Pressure drop correlations: Moody (Darcy-Weisbach) or Hazen Williams
System curve: Comparison between new and aged pipe performance
Pump curve: Verify operating point shift with increased friction
Pipe database: Stainless Steel Pipe (type 13)
1,283 fluid database: Water (PW)
Multi Calc functionality: Scenario comparison (new vs aged conditions)
MINI PRD: P129 — Seawater Cooling NPSH
Status: Plan
Batch: 14 | Priority: P3 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What This Article Does Now
This is a NEW article.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Guide shipbuilding engineers through NPSH analysis for seawater cooling pumps where low suction head (hull-mounted sea chests near waterline) and warm water create cavitation risk. Must open with the reliability challenge (pump cavitation causes failures at sea), explain why NPSH margin matters for naval architecture, then show how FluidFlow calculates NPSHA with seawater properties and sea chest elevation relative to pump.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the naval architecture problem — seawater cooling pumps feed critical machinery (engines, HVAC) but operate with minimal suction head from sea chests, making NPSH margin critical for reliability.

**Pain point focus**: Classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS), pump reliability for propulsion/auxiliary systems, space/weight constraints limiting suction pipe size, tropical water temperature effects.

**FluidFlow capability map**: Liquid Module with seawater fluid properties (salinity, temperature), NPSHA calculation at pump junction, static head from sea chest elevation, pump auto-sizing with NPSH margin verification.

**Practical scenario**: Diesel engine cooling water pump, sea chest 2m below waterline, pump centerline 5m above waterline, 32°C tropical seawater, verify 3m NPSH margin, optimize suction pipe diameter.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting NPSH analysis to at-sea reliability and classification approval.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[NPSH](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#npsh) — in opening statement
[Static Head](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#static-head) — in sea chest elevation discussion
[Vapor Pressure](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#vapor-pressure) — in tropical water section
[Duty Point](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#duty-point) — in pump sizing section
[Suction Specific Speed](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#suction-specific-speed) — in pump selection discussion
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview
SIDEWAYS: P130 Ballast Transfer D Selection, P133 Chiller CW Loops, P122 WFI Loop NPSH, P131 Potable Water Pumps
DOWN (glossary): NPSH, Static Head, Vapor Pressure, Duty Point, Suction Specific Speed
CTA: Contact Us — https://fluidflowinfo.com/contact
Selling Message
6 — "Works across every flow regime" — FluidFlow handles seawater cooling, ballast, fuel, and hydraulic systems from one platform. Weave naturally into shipbuilding application discussion.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Liquid Module: Moody (Darcy-Weisbach) correlation
Physical properties: Seawater (from 1,283 fluid database) with temperature/salinity effects
NPSH calculation: Available at pump junction
Pump auto-sizing: Duty point with NPSH margin verification
Static head: Calculated from sea chest to pump elevation difference
Pipe database: Steel Pipe (type 14) or Copper Pipe (type 6)
Calculation alerts: NPSH margin warnings (from 118 alerts)
MINI PRD: P130 — Ballast Transfer Diameter Selection
Status: Plan
Batch: 14 | Priority: P3 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What This Article Does Now
This is a NEW article.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Guide shipbuilding engineers through sizing ballast transfer piping where fill/discharge times drive diameter selection but space and weight constraints limit options. Must open with the operational challenge (IMO stability requirements + port time limits), explain the velocity-diameter-weight tradeoff, then show how FluidFlow models ballast pump performance against pipe size options to meet time requirements.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the naval architecture problem — ballast systems must fill/empty tanks within port time windows while meeting IMO stability criteria, but oversized piping adds weight and consumes scarce below-deck space.

**Pain point focus**: Classification society approval (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS), IMO MARPOL stability requirements, cargo handling time pressure, steel weight budgets, pipe routing constraints in congested machinery spaces.

**FluidFlow capability map**: Liquid Module with seawater properties, pump curve analysis for flow delivery, system curve comparison for multiple pipe sizes, time-to-fill calculation using volume and flow rate.

**Practical scenario**: 2000 m³ ballast tank, target 90-minute fill time, compare 300mm vs 350mm pipe, calculate pump duty point for each size, verify velocity limits (typically 3.0 m/s max for seawater), analyze weight/space tradeoff.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting ballast pipe sizing to port efficiency and classification approval.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[System Curve](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#system-curve) — in pipe sizing comparison
[Duty Point](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#duty-point) — in pump performance section
[Velocity Limit](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#velocity-limit) — in seawater erosion discussion
[Economic Velocity](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#economic-velocity) — in sizing optimization section
[Pressure Gradient](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#pressure-gradient) — in diameter comparison
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview
SIDEWAYS: P129 SW Cooling NPSH, P132 Fire Main Pressures, P136 Tank Stripping Water C-Values, P131 Potable Water Pumps
DOWN (glossary): System Curve, Duty Point, Velocity Limit, Economic Velocity, Pressure Gradient
CTA: Contact Us — https://fluidflowinfo.com/contact
Selling Message
3 — "Fastest path to validated design" — FluidFlow's pipe auto-sizing and multi-scenario analysis eliminate ballast pipe trial-and-error. Weave naturally into efficiency discussion.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Liquid Module: Moody (Darcy-Weisbach) correlation
Physical properties: Seawater (from 1,283 fluid database)
Pump curve database: Ballast pump vendors
System curve: Interactive chart for diameter comparison
Pipe auto-sizing: Standard velocity or pressure gradient criteria
Pipe database: Steel Pipe (type 14)
Multi Calc functionality: Scenario comparison (300mm vs 350mm)
Configurable warning criteria: Velocity limits (max 3.0 m/s for seawater)
MINI PRD: P131 — Potable Water Pump Sizing
Status: Plan
Batch: 14 | Priority: P3 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What This Article Does Now
This is a NEW article.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Guide shipbuilding engineers through sizing potable water distribution pumps where variable demand (crew size, galley, laundry) and pressure requirements (fixtures on multiple decks) create complex duty point selection. Must open with the regulatory challenge (IMO potable water standards + crew welfare), explain why peak demand vs continuous operation matters for pump selection, then show how FluidFlow models pump curves against system curves for multiple operating scenarios.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the naval architecture problem — ship potable water systems must deliver adequate flow and pressure to all fixtures (galley, laundry, heads) across multiple decks while meeting IMO sanitary standards and crew welfare requirements.

**Pain point focus**: IMO Resolution A.954(23) potable water requirements, classification society rules, crew comfort affecting morale/retention, variable demand from galley/laundry creating pump cycling, multi-deck static head challenges.

**FluidFlow capability map**: Liquid Module with freshwater properties, pump auto-sizing for peak demand, system curve analysis for multiple fixture combinations, static head calculation for multi-deck systems, pressure booster analysis.

**Practical scenario**: 150-crew vessel, 4 decks (20m elevation), size hydrophore pumps for peak demand (galley + laundry + heads simultaneous), verify minimum pressure at top deck fixtures (2.5 bar typical), calculate storage tank volume for demand smoothing.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting potable water system design to crew welfare and classification approval.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Duty Point](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#duty-point) — in pump sizing section
[System Curve](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#system-curve) — in demand scenario analysis
[Static Head](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#static-head) — in multi-deck calculation
[Peak Demand](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#peak-demand) — in fixture loading discussion
[Hydrophore](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#hydrophore) — in pressure maintenance system
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview
SIDEWAYS: P135 Hydrophore Multi-Pump, P129 SW Cooling NPSH, P121 PW Loop Velocity, P126 Duty/Standby Pump Range
DOWN (glossary): Duty Point, System Curve, Static Head, Peak Demand, Hydrophore
CTA: Contact Us — https://fluidflowinfo.com/contact
Selling Message
2 — "System-level thinking" — FluidFlow models all demand scenarios simultaneously, revealing undersizing before installation. Weave naturally into crew welfare discussion.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Liquid Module: Moody (Darcy-Weisbach) correlation
Physical properties: Water (freshwater from 1,283 fluid database)
Pump auto-sizing: Duty point calculation for peak demand flow and head
System curve: Interactive chart for multiple fixture combinations
Static head: Multi-deck elevation calculation
Pipe database: Copper Pipe (type 6) or Stainless Steel (type 13)
Multi Calc functionality: Scenario comparison (different fixture combinations)
Calculation alerts: Pressure warnings at fixtures (from 118 alerts)
MINI PRD: P132 — Fire Main Pressure Distribution
Status: Plan
Batch: 14 | Priority: P3 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What This Article Does Now
This is a NEW article.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Guide shipbuilding engineers through analyzing fire main ring systems where pressure balance at hydrants across the vessel ensures adequate flow during emergencies while meeting SOLAS requirements. Must open with the life-safety challenge (proving all hydrants deliver specified flow/pressure simultaneously), explain why ring main analysis matters for classification approval, then show how FluidFlow models pressure distribution around the ring with multiple discharge points active.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the life-safety problem — SOLAS requires fire main systems to deliver specified flow and pressure to any two hydrants simultaneously, but ring main pressure distribution varies with hydrant location and pump position.

**Pain point focus**: SOLAS Chapter II-2 fire safety requirements, classification society approval (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS), emergency response reliability, ring main balancing complexity, pump redundancy validation.

**FluidFlow capability map**: Liquid Module with ring network analysis, pressure distribution mapping with multiple discharge points active, pump curve modeling for single or dual pump operation, seawater properties with temperature effects.

**Practical scenario**: Ship fire main ring (200m total length), two fire pumps in parallel (one forward, one aft), verify 0.27 MPa (40 psi) minimum at all hydrants with worst-case two-hydrant discharge (100 m³/h each), analyze single-pump degraded mode.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting fire main analysis to life-safety assurance and SOLAS compliance.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)

[Flow Distribution](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#flow-distribution) — in multi-hydrant scenario
[Duty Point](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#duty-point) — in pump sizing verification

SIDEWAYS: P139 Hydrant Ring Main C-Values, P138 Fire Pump Duty, P130 Ballast Transfer D Selection, P143 Dual Pumps in Parallel
DOWN (glossary): Ring Main, Pressure Distribution, System Curve, Flow Distribution, Duty Point
CTA: Contact Us — https://fluidflowinfo.com/contact
Selling Message
2 — "System-level thinking" — FluidFlow models all fire main discharge scenarios simultaneously, revealing SOLAS compliance gaps before sea trials. Weave naturally into life-safety discussion.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Liquid Module: Moody (Darcy-Weisbach) correlation
Physical properties: Seawater (from 1,283 fluid database)
Network analysis: Ring main flow distribution (advanced fluid networks)
Pump curve database: Fire pump vendors
Multi-pump configuration: Parallel pumps with automatic flow balancing
Boundaries: Flow boundaries at hydrants (simultaneous discharge)
Pipe database: Steel Pipe (type 14)
Multi Calc functionality: Scenario comparison (single vs dual pump, different hydrant combinations)
Calculation alerts: Pressure warnings at discharge points (from 118 alerts)
MINI PRD: P133 — HVAC Chiller Cooling Water Loops
Status: Plan
Batch: 14 | Priority: P3 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What This Article Does Now
This is a NEW article.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Guide shipbuilding engineers through designing closed-loop cooling water circuits for HVAC chillers where space constraints, pump sizing, and heat exchanger pressure drops affect system performance. Must open with the comfort/operational challenge (crew welfare + electronics cooling), explain why closed-loop analysis matters for reliable HVAC, then show how FluidFlow models pump selection, heat exchanger losses, and expansion tank sizing for marine chiller systems.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the marine HVAC problem — ship HVAC systems use closed-loop cooling water circuits between chillers and seawater heat exchangers, where pump sizing, heat exchanger pressure drop, and thermal expansion must be balanced in constrained machinery spaces.

**Pain point focus**: Crew comfort affecting morale/productivity, electronics cooling for navigation/comms gear, classification society HVAC requirements, space/weight constraints in machinery rooms, seawater side fouling affecting chiller performance.

**FluidFlow capability map**: Liquid Module with ethylene glycol or water properties, pump auto-sizing for chiller flow requirement, heat exchanger pressure drop modeling (plate or shell-and-tube types), expansion tank sizing using volume calculation, heat transfer for seawater side analysis.

**Practical scenario**: 500 kW chiller (150 m³/h cooling water flow), plate heat exchanger to seawater, calculate cooling water pump duty (head = chiller + heat exchanger + pipe losses), verify 1-2 m/s velocity range, size expansion tank for temperature swing.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting chilled water loop design to HVAC reliability and crew welfare.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Duty Point](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#duty-point) — in pump sizing section
[System Curve](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#system-curve) — in closed-loop analysis
[Heat Exchanger](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#heat-exchanger) — in pressure drop discussion
[Closed Loop](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#closed-loop) — in opening statement
[Expansion Tank](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#expansion-tank) — in thermal expansion section
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview
SIDEWAYS: P129 SW Cooling NPSH, P124 Cooling Water D vs DH, P134 Evaporator Brine Inlets, P131 Potable Water Pumps
DOWN (glossary): Duty Point, System Curve, Heat Exchanger, Closed Loop, Expansion Tank
CTA: Contact Us — https://fluidflowinfo.com/contact
Selling Message
6 — "Works across every flow regime" — FluidFlow handles chilled water, seawater, and refrigerant systems from one platform. Weave naturally into HVAC application discussion.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Liquid Module: Moody (Darcy-Weisbach) correlation
Physical properties: Water or ethylene glycol (from 1,283 fluid database) with temperature effects
Pump auto-sizing: Duty point calculation for chiller flow and total head
Heat exchanger pressure drop: Plate and Frame (P&F) or Shell and Tube (S&T) types with vendor data input
Heat transfer: Fixed heat transfer rate (chiller cooling capacity)
Boundaries: Tank/Vessel Reservoir (expansion tank)
Pipe database: Copper Pipe (type 6) or Steel Pipe (type 14)
Calculation alerts: Velocity limits (from 118 alerts)
MINI PRD: P134 — Fresh Water Generator Brine Inlet
Status: Plan
Batch: 14 | Priority: P3 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What This Article Does Now
This is a NEW article.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Guide shipbuilding engineers through sizing seawater inlet piping to evaporator (fresh water generator) units where high-temperature brine affects pump NPSH and pressure drop calculations. Must open with the operational challenge (potable water production for crew), explain why hot seawater property modeling matters for reliable evaporator feed, then show how FluidFlow models temperature-dependent seawater properties and NPSH at elevated temperatures.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the marine operations problem — fresh water generators (evaporators) heat seawater to 65-80°C to produce potable water, creating high vapor pressure at pump suction and temperature-dependent viscosity affecting pressure drop.

**Pain point focus**: Potable water self-sufficiency requirements, evaporator feed pump cavitation causing downtime, seawater temperature variation (tropical vs cold water routes), classification society requirements, space/weight constraints for suction piping.

**FluidFlow capability map**: Liquid Module with seawater temperature correlation data, NPSHA calculation at elevated temperature (vapor pressure effects), pump sizing with NPSH margin, heat transfer for jacket/heating coil modeling.

**Practical scenario**: Evaporator feed pump (10 m³/h seawater at 75°C), seawater intake 3m below waterline, pump centerline 8m above waterline, calculate NPSHA with hot seawater vapor pressure, verify 3m margin, optimize suction pipe diameter.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting brine inlet design to potable water reliability and crew welfare.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[NPSH](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#npsh) — in opening statement and pump analysis
[Vapor Pressure](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#vapor-pressure) — in elevated temperature discussion
[Static Head](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#static-head) — in seawater intake elevation
[Duty Point](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#duty-point) — in pump sizing section
[Heat Transfer](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#heat-transfer) — in evaporator heating discussion
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview
SIDEWAYS: P129 SW Cooling NPSH, P133 Chiller CW Loops, P122 WFI Loop NPSH, P131 Potable Water Pumps
DOWN (glossary): NPSH, Vapor Pressure, Static Head, Duty Point, Heat Transfer
CTA: Contact Us — https://fluidflowinfo.com/contact
Selling Message
5 — "1,283 fluids including seawater" — FluidFlow's seawater database includes temperature effects on viscosity, density, and vapor pressure. Weave naturally into elevated temperature discussion.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Liquid Module: Moody (Darcy-Weisbach) correlation
Physical properties: Seawater (from 1,283 fluid database) with temperature correlation data (density, viscosity, vapor pressure at 65-80°C)
NPSH calculation: Available at pump junction with elevated temperature vapor pressure
Pump auto-sizing: Duty point with NPSH margin verification
Heat transfer: Fixed heat transfer rate or jacketed vessel element for heating
Static head: Calculated from seawater intake to pump elevation
Pipe database: Copper-Nickel or Steel Pipe (type 14)
Calculation alerts: NPSH margin warnings (from 118 alerts)
MINI PRD: P135 — Hydrophore Multi-Pump Control
Status: Plan
Batch: 14 | Priority: P3 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What This Article Does Now
This is a NEW article.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Guide shipbuilding engineers through modeling hydrophore (pressure tank + multi-pump) systems for potable/sanitary water where variable demand triggers pump staging and pressure tank sizing affects cycle frequency. Must open with the crew welfare challenge (reliable water pressure without pump chatter), explain why pump curve vs system curve analysis matters for staging setpoints, then show how FluidFlow models multiple pumps with different operating scenarios.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the marine operations problem — ship potable water hydrophore systems use pressure tanks and staged pumps to maintain pressure across variable demand, where incorrect pump/tank sizing causes short-cycling or inadequate pressure.

**Pain point focus**: Crew comfort (pressure fluctuations affecting showers/galley), pump reliability (short-cycling reduces bearing life), IMO potable water requirements, classification society rules, energy efficiency for auxiliary systems.

**FluidFlow capability map**: Liquid Module with multiple pump configurations, pump curve analysis for staging (1-pump, 2-pump, 3-pump modes), system curve for demand scenarios, pressure tank sizing using volume/cycling calculations.

**Practical scenario**: Hydrophore with 3 pumps (each 5 m³/h, 5 bar), pressure tank with 200L volume, model pressure decay from 5.0 bar to 3.5 bar cutoff, calculate cycle time for low/medium/high demand, verify pump staging delivers adequate flow without overlapping cycles.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting hydrophore design to crew comfort and pump reliability.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.

MINI PRD: P136 — Tank Stripping Water C-Values
Status: Plan
Batch: 14 | Priority: P3 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What This Article Does Now
This is a NEW article.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Guide shipbuilding engineers through modeling tank stripping systems where small-bore piping and suction nozzles create high pressure drops that affect pump selection and discharge time. Must open with the cargo operations challenge (minimizing residue for IMO MARPOL compliance), explain why accurate C-values or roughness matter for small piping, then show how FluidFlow models stripping pump performance against high-loss suction lines.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the cargo operations problem — tank stripping systems must remove final cargo residue using small-bore suction lines where friction losses dominate, affecting pump sizing and discharge time for MARPOL compliance.

**Pain point focus**: IMO MARPOL Annex I cargo residue limits, stripping time affecting port departure schedules, pump sizing for high suction losses, classification society requirements, steel pipe roughness in bilge/tank bottom areas.

**FluidFlow capability map**: Liquid Module with Hazen-Williams C-values or Darcy-Weisbach roughness, pump curve analysis against high-loss suction system curve, NPSHA calculation for submerged suction, stripping time calculation using tank volume and flow rate.

**Practical scenario**: Ballast tank stripping system (200 m³ residue), 100mm suction line (C=120 for aged steel), submerged pump, calculate stripping flow rate vs pressure drop, verify NPSHA at low tank level, estimate discharge time to meet port schedule.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting stripping system design to MARPOL compliance and port efficiency.

MINI PRD: P137 — Sprinkler Tree Hydraulics
Status: Plan
Batch: 15 | Priority: P3 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What This Article Does Now
This is a NEW article.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Guide fire protection engineers through calculating pressure and flow distribution across sprinkler branch lines (trees) where fitting losses and sprinkler K-factors create uneven discharge patterns affecting NFPA compliance. Must open with the life-safety challenge (proving adequate density at remote area), explain why fitting loss accuracy matters for hydraulic calculations, then show how FluidFlow models sprinkler trees with automatic flow balancing.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the fire protection problem — NFPA 13 requires hydraulic calculation of sprinkler systems to verify adequate flow and pressure at the most remote sprinklers, where branch line fitting losses and sprinkler discharge coefficients create complex flow distribution.
